The Arts in Our Lives
---------------------
### EXPERIENTIAL
[The arts can enhance civil society](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17448689.2014.912479?scroll=top&needAccess=true). Studies show that both audience-based participation and personal participation in the arts are linked to higher levels of civic engagement and respect for different cultures. [One Norwegian study](https://www.parkwestgallery.com/art-and-health-the-benefits-of-viewing-art/) even found a correlation between participating in cultural activities and life satisfaction. This is the basis for [art therapy](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK279641/), which has been linked to significant reductions in depression, anxiety, trauma, and distress, as well as increases in improved mood and self-esteem. Additionally, [a study from the University of Arkansas](https://www.parkwestgallery.com/art-and-health-the-benefits-of-viewing-art/) investigating the impact of students taking field trips to art museums found that 70-88% of students “retained factual information from the tours” and “displayed improved critical thinking skills as well as gains in tolerance and historical empathy following the trip.”
> “Art in all of its manifestations (visual art, music, literature, dance, theater, and more) is an important feature of human societies because it serves as [a cohesive symbolic communicative system](https://www.americansforthearts.org/by-program/reports-and-data/legislation-policy/naappd/partnership-movement-fact-sheet-the-arts-enrich-community-life) conveying cultural norms, history, ideas, emotion, esthetics, and so on.”
>
>
> AMERICANS FOR THE ARTS
### PUBLIC ART
Public art “[humanizes the built environment and invigorates public spaces](https://www.americansforthearts.org/sites/default/files/PublicArtNetwork\_GreenPaper.pdf)” by allowing people to experience art as part of daily life. Art can give communities a [stronger sense of identity](https://www.americansforthearts.org/sites/default/files/PublicArtNetwork\_GreenPaper.pdf); the [St. Louis Arch](https://www.gatewayarch.com/), the giant [heads of Easter Island,](https://mymodernmet.com/easter-island-heads-have-bodies/) and[the High Line in New York City](https://www.thehighline.org/) are defining features and exemplify how the arts are woven into the fabric of a location. Places with dynamic cultural scenes are also attractive, adding to the community’s prosperity. [Two-thirds of businesses consider](https://www.americansforthearts.org/by-program/reports-and-data/legislation-policy/naappd/partnership-movement-fact-sheet-the-arts-enrich-community-life) the arts an important element in making communities attractive places to work.
### TOURISM
Beyond attracting businesses, the arts attract people. The United Nations World Tourism Organization (UNWTO) defines [cultural tourism](https://www.americansforthearts.org/by-program/reports-and-data/legislation-policy/naappd/cultural-tourism-attracting-visitors-and-their-spending) as “movements of persons for essentially cultural motivations such as study tours, performing arts and cultural tours, travel to festivals and other cultural events, visits to sites and monuments, travel to study nature, folklore or art, and pilgrimages.” In recent studies, the global cultural tourism market size was [$5321.7 million](https://www.businessresearchinsights.com/market-reports/cultural-tourism-market-107478#:~:text=The%20global%20cultural%20tourism%20market,14.4%25%20during%20the%20forecast%20period.)in 2022 and is predicted to reach [$11900 million by 2029](https://www.businessresearchinsights.com/market-reports/cultural-tourism-market-107478#:~:text=The%20global%20cultural%20tourism%20market,14.4%25%20during%20the%20forecast%20period.).
#### AMERICA’S WORLD HERITAGE SITES
America’s participation in the United Nations Educational, Scientific, and Cultural Organization ([UNESCO](https://www.unesco.org/en/brief)) helps drive international tourism to the United States. UNESCO designates areas of high cultural or natural value as world heritage sites, almost like the UN’s version of a national park. The United States has 21 [world heritage sites](https://www.visittheusa.com/experience/world-heritage-sites-united-states) like Grand Canyon National Park and the Statue of Liberty.
These heritage sites helped make America the third most popular country for international tourism. In 2023, the US [received](https://www.insidermonkey.com/blog/10-most-visited-countries-in-the-world-in-2024-1260754/?singlepage=1) 79.3 million tourists, second only to France and Spain. Domestic & international travel and tourism in the United States reached a record high in 2023, which added [656,000 jobs, totaling 18 million](https://visaguide.world/news/us-travel-tourism-achieved-record-highs-in-2023-despite-international-challenges/#google\_vignette).
How Does the Creative Economy Function?
---------------------------------------
### CREATORS
When people think about industrialization in the second half of the 19th century, most do not think about art. Nevertheless, the wealth that spread throughout society and expanded the middle class also [stimulated the art market](https://www.learnliberty.org/blog/how-the-free-market-created-impressionism/). People had more time to devote to art, meaning creators had a broader field to work with when trying to understand what was meaningful to consumers and how to create meaningful art out of available resources. More open and expanded markets made possible “[forms of creative individuality](https://fee.org/articles/capitalism-as-art/)” never seen before in history. “The greater the size of the market, the greater the number of artistic forms that [creators](https://fee.org/articles/the-arts-in-a-free-market-economy/) can earn a living from.”
This is essential, especially given that [student debt tends to hit students in the arts and humanities the hardest](https://www.artsy.net/article/artsy-editorial-artists-weathering-student-debt-crisis). According to the Department of Education, 7 out of 10 of the [most expensive schools](https://www.artworkarchive.com/blog/for-love-or-money-an-art-education-in-today-s-economic-climate) are art institutions.
### DISTRIBUTORS
In addition to being producers of their work, [artists are often distributors](https://fee.org/articles/the-arts-in-a-free-market-economy/), turning “their personal visions into material profit by reaching large numbers of eager customers.” [This can empower artists](https://blog.acton.org/archives/81544-can-capitalism-save-the-arts.html) as it gives them more opportunities to thrive, whether that is through technological enhancements and access to instruments and equipment, channels for distribution and marketing like Spotify, Netflix, and [Etsy](https://www.bbc.com/news/business-32310715), or even the amount of time that people can give to creative activities today.
> “For administrators and curators, [the challenge is to advocate and create opportunities for artists,](https://www.americansforthearts.org/sites/default/files/PublicArtNetwork\_GreenPaper.pdf) even while negotiating a minefield of government or institutional bureaucracies, regulations, and budgetary and other constraints, as well as community expectations.”
>
>
> AMERICANS FOR THE ARTS
Artists can also turn to outside support for help distributing their work, which is the focus of [Christopher Vroom](https://www.galeriemagazine.com/spotlight-on-artadia-founder-christopher-vroom/). Vroom founded [Artadia](https://artadia.org/) in 1999, a U.S.-based national nonprofit that “identifies innovative visual artists and supports them with unrestricted, merit-based financial awards and connections to a network of opportunities.” The unrestricted nature of Artadia’s grants allows artists to be independent and keep experimenting while still having the opportunity to form relationships with Artadia’s networks. Vroom also co-founded [Artspace](https://www.artspace.com/) in 2011, an online marketplace to connect artists and their works to collectors and institutions.
The coronavirus pandemic has had an interesting effect on the distribution of art as live events were canceled, art organizations shut their doors, and venues navigated outdoor performances, limited schedules, attendance caps, and social distancing guidelines. Even though very few arts and cultural organizations had used webcasting prior to the pandemic, [many have turned to the Internet to deliver content](https://www.wsj.com/articles/keep-the-virtual-crowds-coming-11616535029) and “now see webcasting as a permanent part of their operations” because it allows people anywhere to access the work of regional organizations, previously known only to residents or local visitors. The pandemic accelerated an evolution in the art world and allowed an increase in access and[immersive online experiences](https://www.artworkarchive.com/blog/how-covid-accelerated-the-evolution-of-the-art-world), redefining the traditional boundaries of art space. The Internet has been “a lifeline” for patrons, donors, and art-lovers, and streaming has expanded audiences.
### BUYERS
These collectors and institutions, both [public and private](http://theconversation.com/public-vs-private-art-collections-who-controls-our-cultural-heritage-80594), also serve as consumers of the creative economy. Public museums include the [Museum of Modern Art](https://www.moma.org/)and the [Metropolitan Museum of Art](https://www.metmuseum.org/) in New York, the [Louvre](https://www.louvre.fr/en) in Paris, [El Museo Nacional del Prado](https://www.museodelprado.es/en/visit-the-museum) in Madrid, the [Capitoline Museum in Rome](https://www.universityofcalifornia.edu/news/origins-public-art-museum), the [Smithsonian](https://www.si.edu/) in Washington, D.C. and New York, and the [Guggenheim](https://www.guggenheim.org/) in New York, Bilbao, Venice, and Abu Dhabi. These public institutions must [adhere to established self-imposed standards and codes of ethics](https://www.nytimes.com/2017/07/25/arts/design/berkshire-museum-art-auction-criticized.html). In the U.S., for example, the [Association of Art Museum Directors’ code](https://aamd.org/about/code-of-ethics)specifically states that art sales cannot provide funds for public institutions “for purposes other than acquisitions of works of art for the collection.” Private museums have significantly more control in selling works and collections.
Additionally, although often overlooked, [families play a large role](https://ifstudies.org/blog/how-flourishing-families-cultivate-great-art). They “can either cultivate great artists or cultivate humans who appreciate art. Both are essential in a thriving society.” This is seen when private citizens invest in art. Art is known as an [alternative investment](https://ruralhandmade.com/blog/art-as-an-alternative-investment-exploring-the-financial#:~:text=Community%20%7C%20People-,Art%20As%20An%20Alternative%20Investment%3A%20Exploring%20The%20Financial%20Potential%20Of,of%20investors%20and%20collectors%20alike.), as opposed to traditional investments of stocks and bonds. Individuals can directly invest in artists, or indirectly invest in art through art investment platforms, where buyers can create and list shares for other investors to buy. Unknown artists’ works can increase in value as they expand their brand, but even the value of well-known works can increase. The value of top artworks rose by an average of 9% annually between 2000 and 2018, while the U.S. stock market rose an average of 3%. U.S. News explains [how to invest in art](https://money.usnews.com/investing/investing-101/articles/how-to-invest-in-art).
The same is true for businesses. In particular, local businesses can positively connect with their communities by buying art for their workplaces, sponsoring art events in the community or offering space for events to take place, or even engaging with local artists when designing programs for their clients or employees. When people are more engaged in cultural production, they tend to offer more support. With more support, there is the opportunity for more market-based cultural production and sustainability.
### PRIVATE ENTERPRISE AND PHILANTHROPY
In the United States, philanthropic contributions totaled close to [$499 billion in 2022](https://philanthropy.indianapolis.iu.edu/news-events/news/\_news/2023/giving-usa-total-us-charitable-giving-declined-in-2022-to-49933-billion-following-two-years-of-record-generosity.html). [Arts philanthropy](https://ssir.org/articles/entry/the\_role\_of\_philanthropy\_in\_advancing\_equity\_in\_the\_arts) specifically in the U.S. generates [about $20 billion annually](https://artsconsulting.com/arts-insights/recent-trends-in-philanthropic-giving-2018/). Even though this is a rather small amount compared to educational, religious, and medical philanthropies, in the visual arts alone there are about 170,000 arts-related nonprofits generating close to $2 billion in economic activity annually. Individual, corporate, and foundation donors make up about 45% of the budget for nonprofit art institutions. About 12% of their income comes from private grants, as opposed to 5% coming from public grants.
The impact results in many impressive developments. [Chicago’s Art on theMart](https://www.artonthemart.com/project-summary) is a permanent public-private partnership that features art casted on the facade of theMart, the largest privately held commercial building in the U.S. In Grand Rapids, Michigan, [ArtPrize](https://www.artprize.org/about) is an “independently organized international art competition” that exhibits art in parks, museums, galleries, and even vacant store fronts. Artists are awarded grants and prizes, decided upon by public vote. Civil society institutions are also a component, such as the [donor conservancy](https://www.centralparknyc.org/governance) that manages and funds Central Park, or the number of privately funded arts museums and festivals across the country from [Michigan](https://www.mackinac.org/how-a-private-arts-festival-thrives) to [Washington, D.C.](https://www.phillipscollection.org/about) to [Utah](https://uaf.org/about-utah-arts-festival).
[A 2017 study looking at arts funding](https://heliconcollab.net/our\_work/not-just-money/) in the U.S. found about 2% of cultural institutions receive more than half of all contributed revenue. Organizations with annual budgets of less than $1 million comprise 90% of nonprofit cultural groups, but only receive 20-25% of funding. The fluidity and synergism of private funding allows quick and creative responsiveness to combat such discrepancies: Many funders are now working with organizations and donors at the community level. For example, the [Mosaic Network and Fund in the New York Community Trust](https://www.nycommunitytrust.org/results/collaborative-funds/#mosaic) brings together arts funders and arts organization leaders in New York City in an effort to connect funders with arts practitioners and help funders understand unique challenges facing artists and cultural organizations in New York City.
Additionally, the vast majority ([close to 85%](https://ssir.org/articles/entry/12\_common\_criticisms\_of\_philanthropyand\_some\_answers)) of giving comes not from large, wealthy foundations but from ordinary citizens who see the needs of their communities differently than policy makers do. This matches [economic arguments](https://www.jstor.org/stable/40229232?seq=1#page\_scan\_tab\_contents) that philanthropy “is part of the implicit social contract that continuously nurtures and revitalizes economic prosperity.”
A 2015 [Philanthropy roundtable poll](https://www.philanthropyroundtable.org/almanac/statistics/national-poll) found 47% of respondents prefer philanthropy over government to solve social problems in America (32% prefer government, 21% undecided); the poll also found 43% of respondents trust charities the most to solve social problems, while 28% trust entrepreneurial companies most and only 14% trust government agencies the most. A strong majority (60%) also believe private charities are more cost-effective than the government (20%). Additionally, as they answer to neither voters nor shareholders, [philanthropies tend to be](https://ssir.org/articles/entry/12\_common\_criticisms\_of\_philanthropyand\_some\_answers) more innovative, efficient, flexible, varied, and personalized in endeavors to address social issues.
#### PHILANTHROPY AROUND THE WORLD
People “acting on their own to improve what they judge to be the common good” is also a [sign of a healthy democracy](https://ssir.org/articles/entry/12\_common\_criticisms\_of\_philanthropyand\_some\_answers), and has long been “[a distinguishing strength of the U.S.](https://www.wsj.com/articles/the-war-on-philanthropy-11578528352)” This is why [in some authoritarian countries](https://www.wsj.com/articles/the-war-on-philanthropy-11578528352), such as Russia, Iran, and China, governments have “clamped down on charities in recent years because they want the state to be the only forum for human influence.”
In 2017, the [European Foundation Center](https://cof.org/organization/european-foundation-centre) (EFC) formed as a group of ten philanthropic organizations from six countries (Denmark, France, Italy, Portugal, Spain, and The Netherlands) after the realization that less government spending on arts and culture gave increased importance to the role of foundations. The EFC further developed an [Arts and Culture Thematic Network](https://philea.eu/events/efc-arts-and-culture-thematic-network-meeting-cultural-welfare-what-role-arts-and-culture-play-in-the-health-and-well-being-of-citizens/), a group of cultural managers from European philanthropic organizations to discuss, share, and gather knowledge about matters that affect funders.
The Role of Government
----------------------
Art is part of a community’s or culture’s collective identity, and for this reason the government is often involved. In the U.S., government involvement in the arts began as part of the New Deal with the [Public Works of Art Project in 1934](https://www.smithsonianmag.com/arts-culture/1934-the-art-of-the-new-deal-132242698/), through which over 3700 artists produced over 15,000 paintings, murals, prints, crafts, and sculptures for government buildings.
